item.title: A truly worthwhile speech/sermon. Why Christian Nationalism isn't Christian

item.title: Calming games
item.description: I enjoy playing No Man's Sky. I know it doesn't have the greatest story ever, but it's relaxing to just drift thru a virtual galaxy discovering new and strange things without a pressing goal.

item.title: I stole this dump, so you don't have to. Tuesday random meme dump of pure chaos
item.description:
1. 10 out of 10 definitely
2. Oooops
3. Looking for a gym buddy
4. Sound ON, it's better
5
6. Me at assassin school
7. Ancient ATM (4391 B.C.)
8. Ah yesss
9. Now that is a way to store your phone on your person
10. Show me that rack baby
11
12
13
14. Sobriety Test?
15. Thank you for making it to the end of the dump. I hope you don't mind me sharing that for the past year and a half, I’ve been working on a game that’s very close to my heart. "Do Not Press The Button (To Delete The Multiverse)" is a quirky adventure crafted with love and passion, with my brother by my side, helping every step of the way. As indie developers, your support means the world to us. By adding our game to your wishlist, you’ll help us reach more players and make a big impact in the gaming community. Here it is: https://store.steampowered.com/app/2336120/Do_Not_Press_The_Button_To_Delete_The_Multiverse/ We can’t do this without you. Thank you for supporting our dream and joining us on this wild adventure!

Look at this face
The cutest newest baby Edit: we did look for the owner, he had no chip until we paid for it. He's had a full work up at the vet and has an appointment for his vaccines later this month.

item.title: Capitalism assumes competition. Allowing the consolidation we've allowed means there's nowhere else to go, and these billion dollar corporations know it. We need heavy handed breakup of near monopolies or it's just going to get worse
item.dc:creator: mynamejosejimenez

item.title: Galaxy inspired glass art
item.dc:creator: ItelyCamel

item.title: Twelve hours ago
item.description: Twelve hours ago I posted a small poem about my older sister Liz, who passed away in August of 2020 very unexpectedly. The outpouring of comments on that post has been emotional. I’m not a writer, but a conversation my mom and I have had a few times in the last four years is what brought it on. My mom has always said that she never cared what happened to herself, but “If something ever happened to my kids I don’t know how I would continue to live.” Losing my sister was her worst nightmare come true, and every day she gets up and continues to live. For Liz, for her grandsons, for the rest of our family. She has told me a few times, that while she knows my sister isn’t alive, she can’t fully tell herself she’s gone. So for my mom and for a small part of me, my sister lives on. She’s at the beach, she’s painting and drinking a hot cup of coffee, or she’s in space seeing planets and stars and the Milky Way is her blanket. But Imgur, even for a brief moment you allowed her to be alive again while you read my poem, and knowing how it ends you mourned her, me and my family and all that we are going through. That is an amazing gift, to feel like she was still alive for a short time and for some to wish her safe travels. Thank you. I won’t be able to show this post or the previous one to my family any time soon but maybe some day my family can read the comments, to know that Liz made people feel something long after she’s gone may bring them some peace as well. Until then, please keep sharing your memories and stories on my post. Grief is exhausting, it’s never ending, but having someone there to understand and sympathize, and empathize has made the journey a little smoother. I hope to see my sister again, I hope my mom sees her too. And I hope you all are reconnected to your loved ones as well.
item.dc:creator: NIMHspal

item.title: Stuff and memes
item.description:
Saw one irl the other day, it's so dumb looking...
Stephen king was definitely a product of his time, but has grown as time has gone on. That's why I respect him.
Hey! Wait a sec!
Never played Elden Ring. I'm bad at soulsbourne games, but the lore seems interesting.
Guys will jerk it anywhere I guess.
Same
I thought I wanted to be Belle, but now I wish I could be Cinderella.
Yeaaaaaaahhhhh, they're referring to a specific 14 words...
No
They're so derpy!!!
If I was still a teacher, there would be no calculators in my class until we learned order of operations and had a day to show them how to use them...
Me in my 30s finally being the real me.
I say this all the time now!!!
Yup!
I have 2 friends that loooove blink, I don't get it, but they need to tale pain killer for their shows. They're both named brit actually...
Yay late stage capitalism!!!!
Ew
Mine is always a piece of shit.
This was from my slut era (it didn't last long)
Beards over stubble any day...

item.title: I used a drone to take a picture of present-day Hiroshima to visualize the size and power of the atomic bomb dropped 79 years ago today
item.description:
Essentially everything in this photo, from left edge to right edge, that wasn't made of concrete and steel was knocked flat. Everything else in the city was set on fire by the heat. The fireball did not touch the ground, so the common idea that people or buildings were "vaporized" is a misconception.
While the Genbaku Dome (Atomic Bomb Dome) and the adjacent park are the de facto memorials to the bombing, the actual hypocenter of the bomb is marked by a single stone block and plaque a few blocks east of the park. It is very innocuous and out of the way.
Close-up of the hypocenter monument.
Originally the Hiroshima Prefectural Industrial Promotion Hall, and now commonly called the Genbaku Dome, Atomic Bomb Dome or A-Bomb Dome (原爆ドーム, Genbaku Dōmu), is part of the Hiroshima Peace Memorial Park in Hiroshima, Japan and was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1996. It a protected monument, and the city has ordinances to protect the sightlines of the park from advertisements and signage.
"Human Shadow Etched in Stone" These stone steps were at the entrance to the Hiroshima Branch of the Sumitomo Bank. Exposed to the atomic explosion the person sitting on the steps waiting for the bank to open is thought to have died on the spot with no possibility of escape. The intense heat of the A-bomb turned the steps whitish; the stone under the sitting person remained dark, like a shadow. The bank is about 1,350 feet from the hypocenter, and this phenomena is often confused with "people being vaporized, only leaving behind a shadow." What was more commonly seen in both Hiroshima and Nagasaki was that materials in shadow against the bomb retained their original color, while the areas outside of shadow were scorched or bleached. I will post more examples of this from Nagasaki in a few days.
Children's Peace Monument within the memorial park. This is a statue of Sadako Sasaki, whose story and her connection to folding paper cranes while she died of leukemia about 10 years after the bombing is a lasting symbol of peace. The cases behind the statue are filled with thousands of paper cranes on strings.
Statue of Sadako upholding an origami crane.
Sadako's original sandals and a personal bag that she carried with her.
Sadako's original paper cranes, made from whatever she could get her hands on while dying in a hospital of cancer caused by nuclear fallout. It never occurred to me that these cranes still existed, or that I would ever see them in person. They are so tiny and delicate and beautiful.
A paper peace crate stuck into the back of the lava rock obelisk at the Trinity Site in New Mexico, which I photographed last year.
Dogtag of James Ryan, an American B-24 crew member who was shot down near Hiroshima in Spring of 1945, and who would die during the atomic bombing in a Japanese POW camp.
Backside of the Atomic Bomb Dome. This is the side that faced the blast, and you can tell that the only remaining pieces of the building are the innermost concrete core; everything facing the explosion was completely shattered and blown away.
A view from above and behind the Dome, about half as high as the fireball would have been. This is a composite image where I tried to remove a protective mesh that obscured the view of the dome. In the upper right is part of the T-shaped bridge that was the intended target of the bomb.
The Peace Memorial itself, with the eternal flame of peace. The flame at the top was lit on August 1, 1964, and has been burning ever since in protest of nuclear weapons, and will continue to burn until there are no nuclear weapons left on earth. I also visited Nagasaki on this trip and I will be posting images from that in a few days. If you are into this stuff, I post way more regularly on my socials under @AtomicAerials as opposed to the seasonal or OTD dumps I make here on Imgur. I'd really enjoy having more people to share this with who would also like to share their thoughts and experiences with me.
item.dc:creator: AtomicAerials
